The kindness shown by the good Shunammite to Elisha was rewarded by the care given to her during the famine. It is wise to anticipate trouble, and when we do so, to protect ourselves if it is lawful to do so. When the famine ended, she returned from the land of the Philistines; that was no longer a suitable place for an Israelite as long as there was a need for it. There was a time when she lived so securely among her own people that she did not need anyone to speak on her behalf to the king; but life is very uncertain, so that things or people we depend on may fail us, and those we think we will never need may befriend us. Sometimes events, small in themselves, prove important, as they did here; for they made the king ready to believe Gehazi's account when it was confirmed. This made him willing to grant her request and to support a life that had been preserved by miracles.

Among other changes in people’s minds caused by suffering, it often gives them different thoughts about God’s ministers, and teaches them to value the advice and prayers of those they have hated and despised. Elisha did not see what Hazael would do in his appearance, but God revealed it to him, which brought tears to his eyes; the more foresight people have, the more grief they are likely to experience. It is possible for a person, under the convictions and restraints of natural conscience, to express great abhorrence of sin, yet later become reconciled to it. Those who are unimportant in the world cannot imagine how strong the temptations of power and wealth are; if they ever attain these things, they will discover how deceitful their hearts are and how much worse than they suspected. The devil ruins people by saying they will certainly recover and do well, thus lulling them into a false sense of security. Hazael’s false account was an injury to the king, who lost the benefit of the prophet's warning to prepare for death, and it was also an injury to Elisha, who would be considered a false prophet. It is not certain that Hazael murdered his master, or if he caused his death, it may have been unintentional. But he was deceitful and later proved to be a persecutor of Israel.

A general idea is given of Jehoram's wickedness. His father undoubtedly taught him the true knowledge of the Lord, but made a mistake by marrying him to the daughter of Ahab; no good could come from uniting with an idolatrous family.

Names do not define character, but it was harmful for Jehoshaphat’s family to take names from Ahab's. Ahaziah's connection to Ahab's family led to his wickedness and downfall. When people choose wives for themselves, they should remember that they are also choosing mothers for their children. Providence arranged it so that Ahaziah would be cut off with the house of Ahab when the measure of their sin was full. Those who share in sinners’ sins must expect to share in their punishments. May all the changes, troubles, and wickedness of the world make us more eager to obtain an interest in Christ's salvation.
